title: Listening - Part 1
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear three different extracts. For questions 1 – 6, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear. There are two questions for each extract.

extracts.context: You hear two people discussing their experiences with urban gardening.
extracts.audio_script: Woman: My balcony is officially overgrown! I think I overdid it with the tomato plants this year. Man: Better that than a sterile concrete slab. [1]I’ve found that even just ten minutes of pruning in the morning sets me up for the day[/1]. Woman: Absolutely. It’s that connection to something living, isn't it? Though [2]I must admit I’ve struggled with the ethical dilemma of what to do about the aphids[/2]. Man: Oh, just use a bit of soapy water. It’s hardly chemical warfare. Woman: I know, but I hate the thought of killing anything. I’ve even started apologizing to the weeds as I pull them out. Man: You’re far too soft, Hollie. I’m quite ruthless with my window box. Survival of the fittest! Woman: Well, I like to think of my balcony as a sanctuary for all creatures, even the annoying ones.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: My balcony is officially overgrown! I think I overdid it with the tomato plants this year.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Better that than a sterile concrete slab. I’ve found that even just ten minutes of pruning in the morning sets me up for the day.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Absolutely. It’s that connection to something living, isn't it? Though I must admit I’ve struggled with the ethical dilemma of what to do about the aphids.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Oh, just use a bit of soapy water. It’s hardly chemical warfare.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I know, but I hate the thought of killing anything. I’ve even started apologizing to the weeds as I pull them out.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: You’re far too soft, Hollie. I’m quite ruthless with my window box. Survival of the fittest!

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Well, I like to think of my balcony as a sanctuary for all creatures, even the annoying ones.

questions.question: What is the main benefit the man gets from gardening?

options.B: A psychological boost in the morning.
options.C: A sense of accomplishment from hard work.

questions.explanation: He says pruning 'sets me up for the day', which refers to a positive mental or psychological start.

questions.question: The woman mentions aphids to illustrate her...

options.A: lack of technical knowledge about pests.
options.B: reluctance to cause harm to living things.
options.C: frustration with the climate in her city.

questions.explanation: She describes her 'ethical dilemma' and says she hates 'the thought of killing anything'.

extracts.context: You hear two professionals discussing the concept of 'unlimited holiday' policies.
extracts.audio_script: Man: Did you see that tech company in Bristol has introduced 'unlimited annual leave'? Woman: I did. [3]It sounds great on paper, but in practice, people often end up taking less time off[/3] than if they had a fixed allowance. Man: Why is that? Surely the freedom is a good thing? Woman: [4]Because there’s no clear benchmark, everyone’s looking at each other to see what’s 'socially acceptable'[/4]. Nobody wants to be the one who looks lazy. Man: I see. So it actually increases peer pressure rather than reducing stress. Woman: Exactly. And without a formal 'use it or lose it' policy, the work just keeps piling up until you feel you can't afford to leave your desk. Man: That’s a shame. I always thought it sounded like the ultimate perk. Woman: It’s a clever branding exercise for the company, but for the staff, it’s a bit of a double-edged sword.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Did you see that tech company in Bristol has introduced 'unlimited annual leave'?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I did. It sounds great on paper, but in practice, people often end up taking less time off than if they had a fixed allowance.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Why is that? Surely the freedom is a good thing?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Because there’s no clear benchmark, everyone’s looking at each other to see what’s 'socially acceptable'. Nobody wants to be the one who looks lazy.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I see. So it actually increases peer pressure rather than reducing stress.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Exactly. And without a formal 'use it or lose it' policy, the work just keeps piling up until you feel you can't afford to leave your desk.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: That’s a shame. I always thought it sounded like the ultimate perk.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: It’s a clever branding exercise for the company, but for the staff, it’s a bit of a double-edged sword.

questions.question: What is the woman's main observation about 'unlimited leave'?

options.A: It leads to a significant increase in absenteeism.
options.B: It results in employees working more than before.
options.C: It is often used as an excuse to cut salaries.

questions.explanation: She says people end up taking 'less time off', which implies they are working more.

questions.question: According to the woman, why do employees hesitate to take holiday under this policy?

options.A: They fear their projects will be reassigned.
options.B: They are uncertain about the expectations of their peers.
options.C: They are too busy with their daily responsibilities.

questions.explanation: She mentions there is 'no clear benchmark' and people look at each other to see what is 'socially acceptable', referring to peer expectations.

extracts.context: You hear two people in Ireland, Emily and Connor, discussing a new art installation in their town.
extracts.audio_script: Man: So, Emily, what do you make of the new sculpture in the town square? Woman: To be honest, Connor, [5]I find it a bit jarring against the traditional architecture of the surrounding buildings[/5]. It’s a bit too 'industrial' for my taste. Man: Really? I love the contrast. [6]I think it’s exactly what the town needs to show we’re looking forward, not just backward[/6]. Woman: I suppose. But couldn't they have chosen something a bit more... organic? It looks like a pile of scrap metal from a distance. Man: That’s the point! It’s a tribute to the town’s manufacturing heritage, just reimagined for the modern age. Woman: Well, I’m glad someone appreciates it. I just hope they didn't spend too much of the council's budget on it. Man: I think it’s an investment in our cultural identity, and you can't really put a price on that.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: So, Emily, what do you make of the new sculpture in the town square?

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: To be honest, Emily, I find it a bit jarring against the traditional architecture of the surrounding buildings. It’s a bit too 'industrial' for my taste.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: Really? I love the contrast. I think it’s exactly what the town needs to show we’re looking forward, not just backward.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: I suppose. But couldn't they have chosen something a bit more... organic? It looks like a pile of scrap metal from a distance.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: That’s the point! It’s a tribute to the town’s manufacturing heritage, just reimagined for the modern age.

dialogue.speaker: woman
dialogue.text: Well, I’m glad someone appreciates it. I just hope they didn't spend too much of the council's budget on it.

dialogue.speaker: man
dialogue.text: I think it’s an investment in our cultural identity, and you can't really put a price on that.

questions.question: What is the woman's main objection to the sculpture?

options.B: Its lack of connection to the local area.
options.C: Its visual incompatibility with its environment.

questions.explanation: She uses the word 'jarring' (clashing) against the 'traditional architecture', indicating a visual mismatch.

questions.question: What does the man believe the sculpture represents for the town?

options.C: A rejection of the town's industrial past.

questions.explanation: He says it shows the town is 'looking forward, not just backward', which is a C1 way of saying progressive/future-oriented.
